3A_Bibliographic.citation: Guerrero, M. D., Barnes, J. D., Walsh, J. J., Chaput, J. P., Tremblay, M. S., & Goldfield, G. S. (2021). Typologies of Family Functioning and 24-h Movement Behaviors. International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health, 18(2), 699.
3A_Bibliographic.journal: International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health
3A_Bibliographic.notes: Author list inferred from DOI metadata.

3B_Sample_Data.abcd_release: Baseline data from the ABCD study (not specified by release number)
3B_Sample_Data.verbatim_sample_description: Participants included 10,712 children (female = 5143, males = 5578) aged 9 and 10 years (M = 9.91, SD = 0.62).

3C_Exposure.exposure_variable: Family typologies derived from latent profile analysis of parental acceptance, parental monitoring, and family conflict
3C_Exposure.exposure_instrument: Child Report of Behavior Inventory (acceptance subscale, 5 items), ABCD-developed parental monitoring scale (5 items), Moos Family Environment Scale conflict subscale (9 items)
3C_Exposure.modalities: N/A - exposure is family functioning typologies, not screen time
3C_Exposure.operationalization: Five latent profiles: P1 (H-acceptance, H-monitoring, L-conflict), P2 (H-acceptance, M-monitoring, M-conflict), P3 (H-acceptance, M-monitoring, H-conflict), P4 (L-acceptance, L-monitoring, M-conflict), P5 (M-acceptance, L-monitoring, H-conflict)
3C_Exposure.reporter: Child-report (acceptance, monitoring); parent-report (family conflict via Moos FES)
3C_Exposure.verbatim_exposure_description: Children's perceptions of parental warmth, acceptance, and responsiveness were assessed using the acceptance subscale (5 items) of the Child Report of Behavior Inventory... Parental monitoring was assessed using a scale (5 items) developed by the ABCD leadership group... Family conflict was assessed using the conflict subscale (9 items) of the Moos Family Environment Scale.
3C_Exposure.notes: This paper examines family typologies predicting movement behavior compliance (PA + screen time + sleep combined). Screen time is part of the composite outcome, not the exposure.

3D_Outcome.primary_outcome: Number of 24-h movement behavior recommendations met (physical activity, screen time <=2h/day, sleep 9-11h/night)
3D_Outcome.outcome_instrument: Youth Risk Behavior Survey (1 item for PA), Youth Screen Time Survey (12 items for screen time), Sleep Disturbance Scale for Children (1 item for sleep)
3D_Outcome.verbatim_outcome_description: For each movement behavior, children were coded as either 1 (meeting the guideline) or 0 (not meeting the guideline) and were then used to create three separate dichotomous variables: (1) 0 recommendations met vs. >=1 recommendation met; (2) 0 recommendations met vs. >=2 recommendations met; (3) 0 recommendations met vs. 3 recommendations met.
3D_Outcome.notes: Screen time is one component of the composite movement behavior outcome (<=2h/day recommendation). Not examined as a standalone outcome.

3E_Design_Analysis.model_type_detail: Latent Profile Analysis (LPA) to derive family typologies; multilevel logistic regression with study site as random intercept to examine associations between family profiles and meeting movement behavior recommendations
3E_Design_Analysis.covariates_listed: age, sex, ethnicity, family income, study site (random intercept)
3E_Design_Analysis.verbatim_analysis_description: Once the best-fitting profile was identified, multilevel logistic regressions were used to examine the association between family profiles and meeting the 24-h guidelines. All models were adjusted for age, sex, ethnicity, and family income and included study site as the random intercept.

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: Family typology P2 (H-acceptance, M-monitoring, M-conflict) vs P1 (H-acceptance, H-monitoring, L-conflict)
3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome: Meeting all 3 movement behavior recommendations (PA, screen time <=2h, sleep 9-11h)
3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: OR for meeting all 3 recommendations vs 0
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Family typology: P2, OR 0.54, 95% CI 0.39, 0.76, p<0.001

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: Family typology P3 (H-acceptance, M-monitoring, H-conflict) vs P1
3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: OR for meeting all 3 recommendations vs 0
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Family typology: P3, OR 0.28, 95% CI 0.20, 0.40, p<0.001

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: Family typology P4 (L-acceptance, L-monitoring, M-conflict) vs P1
3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: OR for meeting all 3 recommendations vs 0
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Family typology: P4, OR 0.24, 95% CI 0.16, 0.36, p<0.001

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: Family typology P5 (M-acceptance, L-monitoring, H-conflict) vs P1
3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: OR for meeting all 3 recommendations vs 0
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Family typology: P5, OR 0.19, 95% CI 0.12, 0.29, p<0.001
Family typology: P2, >=2 Recommendations, OR 0.77, 95% CI 0.63, 0.94, p=0.012
Family typology: P5, >=2 Recommendations, OR 0.35, 95% CI 0.28, 0.44, p<0.001
Family typology: P5, >=1 Recommendation, OR 0.54, 95% CI 0.45, 0.64, p<0.001

3G_Reporting_Quality.notes: Paper reports ORs with CIs for all analyses. Some ORs are quite large (e.g., 0.19 for P5 meeting all 3 recommendations), but these reflect composite movement behavior outcomes, not screen time alone.

3H_Recommendations.recommendation_verbatim: Family interventions aimed at reducing conflict and increasing warmth and monitoring can help to promote the healthy development of the child... Parents/caregivers should focus on instilling healthy habits in children in the early years of their lives as this may be harder as children age.
3H_Recommendations.notes: Recommendations focus on family-level interventions, not screen time reduction per se.

3I_Rhetorical_Moves.modifiable_factor_verbatim: Family interventions aimed at reducing conflict and increasing warmth and monitoring can help to promote the healthy development of the child.
3I_Rhetorical_Moves.discussion_characterization: The discussion frames family functioning as a key determinant of children's movement behaviors. It interprets the progressive decrease in ORs across family typologies as evidence that worse family functioning is associated with lower likelihood of meeting recommendations. The paper notes limitations of subjective measurement and the need for objective screen time assessment. It recommends family-targeted interventions and discusses practical barriers to screen time reduction (e.g., parental reliance on screens to occupy children).
3I_Rhetorical_Moves.ambiguity_notes: Screen time is part of a composite movement behavior outcome (alongside PA and sleep) and is not examined independently. The ORs reflect combined movement behavior compliance, not screen time effects per se. Base rates for meeting each recommendation category are not reported.
